4: A direct revelation: 'When Christ who is your life appears, then you also will appear with him in glory.' Christ is not merely the source of life or the example of life but is himself the believer's life — a participatory identification so radical that the believer's appearance at the parousia is simultaneous with and inseparable from Christ's own appearing. The eschatological hope of Colossians is not individual survival after death but corporate appearing with Christ at his coming — the believer's hidden life (Colossians 3:3) unveiled in glory. The Christological frame encompasses both the hiddenness of the present and the glory of the future.
1: 'If then you were raised with Christ, seek the things that are above, where Christ is, seated at the right hand of God' — resurrection-life is Christological in direction: Christ the risen Lord is located at the right hand of God (the throne-position of Psalms 110:1, fulfilled in the resurrection-exaltation, Acts 2:33-35). The community has been raised with him (Colossians 2:12); their orientation must therefore match his location. 'Seek the things above' is not escapism but attention to the Christological reality that is more real than what is visible below.
2: 'Set your minds on things above, not on things that are on earth' — the Christological imagination: the 'things above' are defined by Christ's location and character (Colossians 3:1: where Christ is seated). To 'set your mind' (phroneite) on things above is to habituate the imagination toward Christ's reality; this is the renewal of the mind (Romans 12:2) applied through the Christological lens of the letter. The earthly things are not evil in themselves but cease to be the constituting frame of the community's self-understanding.
3: 'For you died, and your life is now hidden with Christ in God' — the community's true existence is located in God through Christ: their biography has been relocated from the world's frame to the divine frame. 'Hidden with Christ in God' (kekryptai syn tō Christō en tō theō) — the perfect tense: completed and remaining hidden. Their social self (what the world sees and judges) is no longer their ultimate identity; their real life is where Christ is, secured within God himself. This is the deepest Christological anti-anxiety statement in the letter.
5: 'Put to death therefore what is earthly in you: sexual immorality, impurity, passion, evil desire, and covetousness, which is idolatry' — the 'therefore' grounds the ethical imperative in the Christological indicative of Colossians 3:1-4: because you have died with Christ and your life is hidden in God, enact that death experientially. The catalogue of vices corresponds to the old self under the dominion of darkness (Colossians 1:13); their mortification is the ongoing application of the baptismal Christ-death. Covetousness as idolatry: it substitutes a created good for the Creator, which is the very structure of anti-Christological existence.
6: 'Because of these things the wrath of God is coming' — the eschatological judgment that Christ himself taught (Matthew 25:31-46) and that Paul regularly invokes as the horizon of ethical seriousness. The 'wrath of God' is not arbitrary punishment but the necessary response of holiness to persistent idol-worship; Christ who absorbed the wrath on the cross (Romans 3:25; 1 John 2:2) is also the Christ who will execute judgment at the parousia (2 Corinthians 5:10). The community that has been rescued from the wrath (1 Thessalonians 1:10: Jesus who delivers us from the coming wrath) is called to live accordingly.
7: 'In these you too once walked, when you were living in them' — the pre-Christ biography: the 'once' (pote) temporal marker is the Pauline before/after Christological contrast. The community once walked in the ways of the dominion of darkness (Colossians 1:13); they have been transferred to the kingdom of the Son. The old walk is not their present identity; it is history, displaced by the Christ-event that relocated their life (Colossians 3:3).
8: 'But now you must put away all such things: anger, rage, malice, slander, and obscene talk from your mouth' — the 'but now' (nyni de) is the Christological temporal turning point: the transfer to Christ's kingdom (Colossians 1:13) means a new behavioral regime. The vices listed are the expressions of the hostile mind (Colossians 1:21) — the pre-Christ condition of enmity toward God and neighbor. Christ-transformation produces the opposite: the compassion, kindness, and love of Colossians 3:12-14.
9: 'Do not lie to each other, since you have taken off the old self with its practices' — the aorist 'have taken off' (apekdysamenoi) identifies the stripping of the old self with the baptismal event (Colossians 2:12); it is completed action, not an ongoing project. The old self is the self under the dominion of the flesh and death, the Adamic self that was oriented away from God; its practices were the vices of Colossians 3:5-8. The new community lives from the completed reality of the Christ-death already applied to the old self.
10: 'And have put on the new self, which is being renewed in knowledge according to the image of its Creator' — Christ is the image of God (Colossians 1:15); the new self is being renewed into conformity with Christ — who is himself the image. To be 'renewed in the image of the Creator' is to be renewed into the image of Christ, the new Adam (Romans 5:14; 1 Corinthians 15:45). The eschatological transformation is in process: the perfect 'put on' (completed event) combined with the present 'being renewed' (ongoing process) describes the already-not-yet structure of Christological formation.
11: 'Here there is no Greek or Jew, circumcised or uncircumcised, barbarian, Scythian, slave or free — but Christ is all and in all' — the most compressed Christological summary in the letter: 'Christ is all and in all' (panta kai en pasin Christos). Every social, ethnic, and religious distinction that the old world used to organize reality has been superseded; Christ — not those categories — constitutes the community's identity. All is summed up in Christ (Ephesians 1:10); he is the measure, content, and frame of everything that matters.
12: 'Therefore, as God's chosen ones, holy and beloved, put on compassionate hearts, kindness, humility, meekness, and patience' — the virtues are the character of Christ applied to community life. Christ himself embodied each: compassion (Matthew 9:36: moved with compassion for the crowds), kindness (Matthew 11:30: my yoke is easy), humility (Matthew 11:29: meek and lowly in heart), meekness (the beatitude, Matthew 5:5), patience (Hebrews 12:1-2: with endurance he ran the race). To 'put on' these virtues is to put on Christ (cf. Galatians 3:27; Romans 13:14).
13: 'Bearing with one another and, if one has a complaint against another, forgiving each other; as the Lord has forgiven you, so you also must forgive' — the Christological ground of forgiveness: the community forgives 'as the Lord has forgiven you' (kathōs kai ho Christos charisato hymin, using Christos explicitly). The quality and extent of Christ's forgiveness — unlimited (Colossians 1:14: forgiveness of all sins), unearned (Colossians 1:21: while enemies), permanent — sets the standard for the community's forgiveness of one another. No offense within the community can exceed what Christ has already forgiven.
14: 'And above all these put on love, which binds everything together in perfect unity' — love as the outer garment that completes and holds together all the other virtues. Christ himself is the love of God enacted in the world (Romans 5:8: God demonstrates his love in that while we were still sinners Christ died for us); the community that 'puts on love' is putting on the character that Christ is and the act that Christ performed. The 'bond of unity' (syndesmos tēs teleiotētos) that love creates mirrors Christ's own role as the one 'in whom all things hold together' (Colossians 1:17).
15: 'And let the peace of Christ rule in your hearts, to which indeed you were called in one body. And be thankful' — the peace of Christ (hē eirēnē tou Christou) is the reconciliation that Christ accomplished (Colossians 1:20: making peace by the blood of his cross). This peace — objectively achieved at the cross — is to be the internal governor of the community's decision-making and relational life; where there is conflict, the community returns to the reality of the cross-peace. The 'one body' recalls the body-of-Christ ecclesiology: they were called into the one body of Christ, which is itself defined by his peace.
16: 'Let the word of Christ dwell in you richly, teaching and admonishing one another in all wisdom, singing psalms and hymns and spiritual songs, with thankfulness in your hearts to God' — the 'word of Christ' (ho logos tou Christou) is both the message about Christ and the teaching of Christ himself; it is to inhabit the community richly (plousiōs), producing wisdom, mutual formation, and worship. The psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs are Christological praise: the community fills itself with Christ's word and responds with Christ-directed song (the Colossian hymn of Colossians 1:15-20 may exemplify exactly this).
17: 'And whatever you do, in word or deed, do everything in the name of the Lord Jesus, giving thanks to God the Father through him' — the comprehensive Christological claim on every action: 'in the name of the Lord Jesus' means in the authority, character, and identity of the Lord Jesus. Nothing is secular, nothing is too mundane for Christological enactment; every word and act is to be consistent with who Christ is and what he has done. The thanksgiving through him is the Christological mediation of all worship: access to God is through Christ.
18: 'Wives, submit to your husbands, as is fitting in the Lord' — the Christological qualifier 'in the Lord' (en kyriō) transforms the household code's framework: the submission called for is not the world's submission (the Roman hierarchical domination-compliance system) but the Christ-shaped submission that mirrors the mutual deference of the body of Christ. 'As is fitting in the Lord' means the manner and motivation of the submission are governed by Christ's own character — not compulsion but the free self-giving that Christ himself modeled.
19: 'Husbands, love your wives, and do not be harsh with them' — the love commanded is agapaō — the Christ-love defined by self-giving (Ephesians 5:25: husbands, love your wives as Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her). Harshness (pikrainō) is incompatible with the Christ-love: Christ who was gentle and lowly in heart (Matthew 11:29) is the standard against which the husband's treatment of his wife is measured. The brevity of the command is striking: no theological elaboration, just the Christological norm applied directly.
20: 'Children, obey your parents in everything, for this pleases the Lord' — the Lord (Christ) sets the approval standard for family relationships. Obedience to parents is not merely cultural obligation or Roman household management but Christological approval: Christ himself was obedient to his earthly parents (Luke 2:51: he went down with them and came to Nazareth and was submissive to them) and to his heavenly Father (Philippians 2:8: obedient to the point of death). The child who obeys participates in the obedience-pattern that Christ himself embodied.
21: 'Fathers, do not provoke your children, lest they become discouraged' — the Christological reflex: Christ welcomed children and identified with the vulnerable (Mark 10:14: let the children come to me; Matthew 18:3-4: unless you become like children). To embitter or provoke children contradicts the Christ-character that the father is supposed to embody (Colossians 3:12: clothe yourselves with compassion, kindness, humility). The Christ who is gentle with the weak demands a corresponding gentleness from fathers toward those in their care.
22: 'Slaves, obey your earthly masters in everything; not by way of eye-service, as people-pleasers, but with sincerity of heart, fearing the Lord' — the Christological reframing of slave-labor: 'fearing the Lord' (phoboumenoi ton kyrion) identifies Christ as the ultimate authority and audience for all work. The 'not as people-pleasers' (mē en ophthalmodouliais) addresses the structural problem of slave-labor: slaves worked when watched, relaxed when not. The Christ-fear inverts this: the slave works with integrity because the Lord Christ is always watching, and the earthly master's gaze is irrelevant to the ultimate quality standard.
23: 'Whatever you do, work at it with your whole heart, as working for the Lord, not for human masters' — the repetition of the Christ-as-employer frame intensifies the point: every task the slave performs — no matter how menial, no matter how invisible to the earthly master — is done before the Lord Christ. This is not just motivation management; it is a Christological ontology of work: all legitimate labor is service to Christ, and Christ receives it as such. The 'whole heart' (ek psychēs) means nothing is held back from the Lord's service.
24: 'Since you know that from the Lord you will receive the inheritance as your reward. You serve the Lord Christ' — the Christological reversal of the slave's situation: under Roman law, slaves had no legal inheritance; under Christ's lordship, they will receive an inheritance from the Lord as a reward. 'Lord Christ' (tō kyriō Christō) combines the two titles in a single phrase: he is the cosmic sovereign (Lord) and the anointed fulfiller of all promise (Christ). The slave who serves him serves the highest authority in the universe and will receive the inheritance that human law denied him.
25: 'For the wrongdoer will be paid back for the wrong he has done, and there is no partiality' — the Christological justice: the eschatological judge (Christ, 2 Corinthians 5:10: we must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ) shows no partiality (ou prosōpolēmpsia). This no-partiality principle cuts both ways in the slave-master context: the slave who wrongs the master will be judged, but so will the master who wrongs the slave (Colossians 4:1). The entire system of human hierarchy is flattened before the one judge who sees with perfect equity.
